posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
I’ll save you looking through all the blurb - this is a great wine, one of the best I’ve enjoyed this year.

I suppose this is a bit like Neal M’s post about Pavie, except with the opposite outcome. I’ve tasted countless vintages of SM since the 82 and never been a fan, although I love many similar wines. I did admire, rather than love, the 03 tasted earlier this year, but this wine is on another level entirely.

We had a discussion about the Cuvée Jean Gautreau back in May, when I was toying with the idea of buying some, so I won’t repeat the whole story behind it.

I opened it in the morning, but in the first glass, the bouquet was fairly muted - the attack was similarly unexceptional, rather simple even. So I decanted it and the wine took off: it started displaying notes of violet, cassis and a little plum, further notes of tobacco and leather emerging in the background. The attack brightened up, with notes of cassis and plum, but in a style which was more Margaux than Pauillac, building into an incredibly lingering finish, dominated by raspberry. If you have ever seen someone blowing smoke rings - well, this is similar - the rings of raspberry lift around the top of the palate and then waft away into the moonlight.
As the hours passed, so the finish became longer, the fruit more persistent, but never unsubtle.
My experience of 1st Growths is limited but this, for me, is 1st Growth standard.
95 pts and I’m probably being conservative.

It’s hard to actually pinpoint the precise style - this is not like a Pauillac, nor a St.Julien, for me it tastes more like a Margaux, perhaps with a touch of Moulis and a pinch of Pessac.

Anyway, if you only like Pavie-type wines, look away. It’s quite possible to like both, of course. But this is quintessential Bordeaux for connoisseurs of subtle, refined wines which wear their fruit like a Chanel dress - no need for the vulgarity of a plunging neckline.

I only bought one 01 and will certainly buy more, but looking around I was surprised this morning to see how easy it is to find the Cuvée at prices which are really not that high.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: So this is a great post to wake up to, as I sit here on a mini-vacation overlooking the ocean, sitting on the water with my dog. I love the regular bottling of the 2001 vintage, but have never had this special cuvée in 2001. I really, obviously, I need to track this baby down. Thanks for the post!

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked: Cheers - well, this is just your sort of wine, obviously. Basically it’s like Sociando-Mallet (and I forgot to mention the typical hint of greenness) but more intense and complex. I really enjoyed the Malescot 04 last weekend and bought some more as a result, but I couldn’t help thinking back to it when I was drinking this - because the SM Cuvée JG is so much better. It’s not that it’s old-fashioned, because class and finesse never went out of fashion, it’s just exceptionally good. Even at 60€, it’s bloody good value, too.

posts.cooked:

There are no absolutes in fashion or winemaking:

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
I’ve still never tasted any examples of this cuvee.

Some of have come up at auction, but I’ve never stepped up.

This sounds very good though.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: 1998 is one well worth seeking out. A bit more satiny and charming than the 2001.

link_counts.title: Tempting wine purchases you’ve passed on—give others a shot

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked: Thanks for the tip, William, I’ll look out for that one. I did find some 2010, which sounds interesting since it’s 100% Cabernet Sauvignon.

posts.name: Claus Jeppesen
posts.cooked: I believe a case was released containing 12 bottles Cuvee Jean Gautreau of each vintage 1995 -2006.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked: Sounds like a perfect Christmas present to myself! Excellent idea - I shall have to write to Santa Claus! Actually, I believe there have been a few verticals organized over the years. FYI, Neal Martin has one in his rather long pipeline which will be published on Vinous, which will be very interesting.

posts.name: Kelly Flynn
posts.cooked:
Thanks for the note, Julian. No surprise on the quality of the 01 Jean Gautreau. I am generally a SM fan, though have only had the pleasure of the 03 JG – and indeed what a pleasure it was!

At the price points I’m seeing, I think they are all steals.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked: Thanks Kelly - presumably you have tasted both the SM and the Cuvée Jean Gautreau 03s? How would you say the latter differed and is it worth the extra cost? I’m guessing that it is but it would be great to have your opinion.

posts.name: Kelly Flynn
posts.cooked:
I thought 03 JG was a discernible step up in quality – bigger, more polished – and I really enjoyed the normale!

The one caveat I would offer is that, as is often the case with the “luxury cuvees”, arguably something gets lost in the up sell. In other words, some of what makes SM idiosyncratic and admired – austerity, rusticity, bell pepper – is perhaps diminished in the fancier version. Personal taste.

posts.name: Jason L Jones
posts.cooked: I had the 96 at Table Donkey and Stick in Chicago and it was tremendous. I don’t see it that often at the places I buy wine or I’d have more in my cellar.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
I thought 03 JG was a discernible step up in quality – bigger, more polished – and I really enjoyed the normale!

The one caveat I would offer is that, as is often the case with the “luxury cuvees”, arguably something gets lost in the up sell. In other words, some of what makes SM idiosyncratic and admired – austerity, rusticity, bell pepper – is perhaps diminished in the fancier version. Personal taste.

Thanks Kelly - I suspect that’s why I liked it!

Jason - there isn’t a lot of it around. I had never seen any at all and only vaguely heard of it, but apparently it was only sold at the château until 2013 or thereabouts, which would explain it.

posts.name: Claus Jeppesen
posts.cooked:
Sounds like a perfect Christmas present to myself! Excellent idea - I shall have to write to Santa Claus! Actually, I believe there have been a few verticals organized over the years. FYI, Neal Martin has one in his rather long pipeline which will be published on Vinous, which will be very interesting.

Attach this to your letter to Santa

Jean Gautreau.jpeg1748×1002 228 KB

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
That really would be a mighty cool thing to have!

Sadly, there are none left - I found the name of the négociant (SOBOVI) who sells them, who I spoke to this morning. The case was a limited edition which has sold out. All the wines however are still available. They promised to send me a list of retailers: quantities are so limited that not many actually stock the wines - if you want some, they have to make an order, but apparently this can be done. I’ll report back when I know more.

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked:
I just tried the 1997 Gautreau and it’s beautiful. There is still a good amount of tannin but it drinks very well and has lovely fruit in addition to the slight green streak of Sociando.

Our bottle of 1996 Gautreau was slightly corked and otherwise seemed very backward. I won’t open another of those for a long time, but I’m considering picking up some more of the 97.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked: Thanks for posting, Craig - the 97 sounds excellent - I’ll look out for some. I was considering trying a 2004 in coming weeks, but that sounds a little premature.
